quizzes.title: Under Construction
quizzes.subtitle: Unfinished Trails in the US
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: When finished, What trail would be United State's longest Rail Trail?
questions.answerText:
The Cowboy Trail currently runs 195 miles across northern Nebraska, following the abandoned Chicago & Northwestern rail route. The railroad called this the "Cowboy Line."

When complete, the trail will connect the Nebraska towns of Chadron and Norfolk, a length of 321 miles (517 km), making it the longest rails-to-trails conversion in the United States.

questions.questionText: What long distance hiking trail aims to stretch across North America, Europe and into Africa?
questions.answerText:
The "International Appalachian Trail" or "Sentier international des Appalaches",  was originally a hiking trail which ran from Katahdin Woods and Waters National Monument in Maine through New Brunswick, and on to the Gaspé Peninsula of Quebec, after which it followed a ferry route to  Newfoundland, and then continued to the northern-easternmost point of the Appalachian Mountains at Belle Isle

As of July 2022, there are widely geographically dispersed IAT-branded walking trails in Greenland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Scotland, Northern Ireland, Ireland, Isle of Man, Wales, England, Spain, Portugal, and Morocco.

questions.questionText: What trail project aspires to provide an off-road cycle and walking trail stretching from Washington DC to Washington State?
questions.answerText: The Great American Rail-Trail is a planned cross-country rail trail that will run 3,700 miles (6,000 km) between Washington D.C. in the east and the state of Washington in the west. The trail is already more than 53% complete, with over 2,000 completed miles on the ground. It runs through 12 states and the District of Columbia, and will be within 50 miles (80 km) of 50 million Americans.  The work is being facilitated by Rails-to-Trails Conservancy. The Great American Rail-Trail is composed of over 150 existing multi-use trails with about 90 gaps to be filled.
questions.answerImageCaption: Keystone Viaduct on the Great American Rail Trail in PA

questions.questionText: What coastal trail project aims to unite 15 states and 450 cities.
questions.answerText:
In 1991 a group of cyclists met to promote the idea of a cycling route stretching along the entire East Coast of the US.  Five years later the first five segments were designated.

When completed the East Coast Greenway will stretch from  Key West, Florida to the Canadian boarder in Maine, connecting 15 states and 450 towns and cities.

In 2024, the route reached over 1,050 miles and is 36% complete.

questions.questionText: What Triple-Crown-of-Hiking Trail is the only one still under active expansion?
questions.answerText:
The Continental Divide trail stretches across the United States some 3,100 miles between the borders of Mexico and Canada.

In 2021, the CDT was about 70 percent complete, with a combination of dedicated trails and dirt and paved roads.

quizzes.title: Trail Food
quizzes.subtitle: What we eat in the woods
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What traditional, long-lasting trail-food typically contains beef, suet, and dried fruit?
questions.answerText:
Pemmican is a mixture of tallow, dried meat and, and sometimes dried berries.  This greasy treat was an important part of indigenous cuisine and it is still prepared today. The name comes from the Cree word "pimi", meaning "fat".

Early explorers and hikers, relied on pemmican because it is calorie-dense and shelf-stable.

questions.questionText: What must-have drink mix was popular on the early Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText:
Cheap, light-weight, and long-lasting, bouillon cubes were once a common defense against dehydration on the trail.  

When media sensation, Emma "Grandma" Gatewood made history as the first woman to hike the Appalachian Trail, Sports Illustrated reported that:

"For snacks, Grandma nibbled wild huckleberries, young sorrel for salad and sucked bouillon cubes to combat loss of body salt."

questions.questionText: What indestructible snack was once considered a trail staple?
questions.answerText:
Hardtack gets its name from the word "tack", the British sailor slang for "food".

Made from flour, water, and salt, hardtack is so hard that it requires moisture to make it palatable.   What it lacks in flavor, it makes up for in calories and durability.

Hardtack and smoked bacon where a common meal-combo for early trekkers like American naturalist and writer, John Muir.

questions.questionText: What does GORP stand for?
questions.answerText:
Sweet and salty, GORP is cheap and easy.

Although the term "Trail Mix" is gaining in popularity, good ole raisins and peanuts, is one of of the few trail foods to earn its own acronym.
questions.answerImageCaption: Good Ole Raisins and Peanuts, mixed with other stuff.

questions.questionText: What is Australian trail mix called?
questions.answerText: Scroggin, or the less common term "schmogle", is Australian for GORP. Although it does seem that the term "Trail Mix" is gaining in popularity.

quizzes.title: Hiking Movies
quizzes.subtitle: Watching other people Hike
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What movie is based on Bill Bryson's humorous account of hiking the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText: A Walk in the Woods is a 2015 movie starring Robert Redford, who plays author Bill Bryson.  The movie and book recount Bryson's attempt to hike the Appalachian Trail, with his irreverent and out-of-shape friend.
questions.answerImageCaption: A Walk in the Woods movie poster

questions.questionText: What movie tells the true story of one woman's 2700-kilometer trek across the Australian desert accompanied only by a dog and four camels?

choices.text: The Adventures of Priscilla, Queen of the Desert

questions.answerText: Starring Mia Wasikowska and Adam Driver, Tracks tells the story of Robyn Davidson who set out from Alice Springs to trek across 2700 kilometers of harsh desert of Western Australia to reach the ocean.  Davidson had no other purpose than to reach the ocean and find herself on a journey of self-discovery

questions.questionText: What movie, starring Martin Sheen tells the story of a father carrying his son's ashes on the Camino de Santiago.
questions.answerText:
Directed by his son Emilio Estevez, Martin Sheen plays a father who decides to walk the Camino de Santiage to fulfil his son's wish of finishing the hike, while carrying his ashes.  

The Way was release in 2010 and received a Film Critics' Award

questions.questionText: What 2014 movie,  based on Cheryl Strayed's account of her hike on the Pacific Crest Trail, stars Reese Witherspoon?
questions.answerText:
Wild recounts Cheryl Strayed's hike on the Pacific Crest Trail.   

Despite her lack of hiking experience, Cheryl takes off on an1100-mile hike in an attempt to deal with the pain of her mother's death and her own heroin abuse.

questions.questionText: What 2015 independent documentary about thru-hiking the Appalachian Trail is often considered to be one of the best portrayals of thru-hiker culture?

choices.text: The Long Start to the Journey

questions.answerText: The Long Start to the Journey follows filmmaker Chris Gallaway on his personal attempt to thru-hike the Appalachian Trail and to learn what the trail means to individuals he meets along the way. Chris (or 'Frost' as he comes to be known on the trail) meets hikers young and old with wildly disparate backgrounds, 'trail angels' who provide food and support along the way, and an assortment of wildlife that make the journey much more exciting. This is a personal story of struggle and perseverance, as well as a historical account of the origins and cultural relevance of the Appalachian Trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Long Start to the Journey

quizzes.title: Starts and Stops
quizzes.subtitle: Where great trails begin and end
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What famous long distance trail ends in Canada for north bound hikers, but starts in the United States for south bound hikers?
questions.answerText: North bound hikers on the Pacific Crest trail are free to cross the border into Canada.  However, it is illegal to cross in the southern direction from Canada into the US.
questions.answerImageCaption: The "start" of the PCT on the US-Canada border

questions.questionText: Even before the Long Trail was blazed, it was billed as a trail stretching from one end of Vermont to the other.  However, for the first 20 years the trail fell short of reaching the Canadian border.  What was the original northern terminus of the Long Trail?
questions.answerText: For the first twenty-ish years, the Long Trail started at the Massachusetts border and ended on Jay Peak.  This bothered Roy Buchanan, who founded the Long Trail Patrol.  In the summer of 1930, he and a few others extended the trail another eight miles to its current terminus on the Canadian border.
questions.answerImageCaption: Buchanan boys at the 592 marker on the Canadian border

questions.questionText: What long distance hiking challenge is an amalgam of other famous hiking trails and begins and ends at the Grand Canyon?
questions.answerText:
The Great Western Loop is a 6,875 mile-long hiking route that links five long-distance hiking trails: the Pacific Crest Trail, the Pacific Northwest Trail, the Continental Divide Trail, the Grand Enchantment Trail, and the Arizona Trail. It traverses the Mojave Desert, the Sonoran Desert, 12 National Parks, and 75 wilderness areas.

The first person the hike the entire route was Andrew Skurka, in 2007.  Skurka began and ended the route at the Grand Canyon, averaging 33 miles per day.
questions.answerImageCaption: Andrew Skurka, the first person to complete the Great Western Loop

questions.questionText: What long distance trail starts at a beach in Delaware and ends at a beach  in California?
questions.answerText: The American Discovery Trail is a system of recreational trails and roads that collectively form a coast-to-coast hiking and biking trail across the mid-tier of the United States.   This 6800 mile route starts in Cape Henlopen State Park, and ends at the Point Reyes National Seashore,

questions.questionText: What trails begins and ends at a junction with the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText: Originally intended as an alternative route for the Appalachian Trail, the southern terminus of the Tuscarora Trail is at a junction with the Appalachian Trail in Shenandoah National Park in Virginia.  The northern terminus of the Tuscarora Trail is at another junction with the Appalachian Trail, plus the Darlington Trail, on Blue Mountain about 10 miles north of Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.
questions.answerImageCaption: A stone marker at the southern terminus of the Tuscarora Trail

quizzes.title: Appalachian Trail Towns
quizzes.subtitle: 56 Officially Designated Communities along the AT
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What AT community features artesian wells in the middle of town?
questions.answerText:
After passing the historic Boiling Springs Tavern, AT Hikers pass the famous artesian wells that bubble and appear to be boiling.  

Boiling Springs is also home to the Appalachian Trail Conference, the organization that officially recognizes AT Trail Communities.
questions.answerImageCaption: Boiling Springs Tavern, Boiling Springs, PA

questions.questionText: What Vermont AT Town is also the home of the King Arthur Baking Company?
questions.answerText:
Norwich Vermont is a small town on the banks of the Conneticut River.   While it attracts its share of hikers, the town is better known as a mecca for home bakers, as it is the home of the King Arthur Baking Company, formerly the King Arthur Flour Company.

Founded in 1790, the King Arthur Baking Company  is a supplier of flour, ingredients, baking mixes, cookbooks, and baked goods. It also runs two baking schools, one at its Norwich, Vermont bakery and the other in Burlington, Washington.
questions.answerImageCaption: King Arthur Baking Company Store Interieor

questions.questionText: What Virginian AT Town  is also known as "Trail Town USA"
questions.answerText: Damascus is a small town in Southwest Virginia with a big claim to fame: Seven nationally known trails intersect within town limits, making it “Trail Town USA”. Originally a railroad town in the lumber boom of the early 20th century, this small-town economy reinvented itself through outdoor recreation and tourism in the 1990s and has been thriving ever since. Tourism is one of the largest economic drivers in Washington County, VA, second only to agriculture.
questions.answerImageCaption: Damascus Downtown with Blaze on Pole

questions.questionText: The Appalachian Trail passes through  only three downtowns, Damascus, VA, Hot Springs, NC, and this town.
questions.answerText:
The Appalachian Trail runs down Main Street of Hanover, NH. It is the first town that southbound hikers pass through on their journey and the last for northbounders.

Hanover is home to Dartmouth College, an Ivy League university founded in 1769. Some trails in the extensive trail network created by the Dartmouth Outing Club in the early 20th century became the A.T. in the Upper Valley in 1929. The DOC maintains​ 50 miles of the A.T. between Hanover and Woodstock, NH.

questions.questionText: What AT Town is also home to the Nation's oldest monument to George Washington?
questions.answerText: Those hiking along Maryland's South Mountain, have the opportunity to climb and enjoy the view from the top of the oldest monument erected tin honor George Washington.  The tower was constructed in 1825, 58 years before the completion of the monument in Washington DC.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Washington Monument on South Mountain in Boonsboro, MD

quizzes.title: Hall of Fame Rail Trails
quizzes.subtitle: The nation's best rail trails according to the Rails to Trails Conservancy
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What 13-mile trail across Vermont's Lake Champlain features a bike ferry?
questions.answerText:
The Island Line Trail follows the route of the Island Line Railroad, built by the Rutland Railroad in 1901.  The trail runs along a causeway over Lake Champlain, the 6th largest lake in the US.  

During the summer, Local Motion, a  Vermont based non-profit dedicated to getting more people on bikes, operates a bike ferry traversing a 200 foot gap in the causeway.
questions.answerImageCaption: Crossing Lake Champlain on the Island Line Trail

questions.questionText: What 63-mile Iowa rail trail features 72 bridges and passes ruined boxcars lying in the river where they derailed in the 1920's
questions.answerText:
Once part of the Wabash Railroad's Omaha to St. Louis rail line, the Wabash Trace Nature Trail is a 63-mile rail trail located in Iowa which stretches from the city of Council Bluffs southeast to the city of Blanchard.   

On July 20, 1928 one of the worst wrecks on the Wabash Railroad happened about a mile east of Mineola.  The 115-foot long trestle bridge over Lone Tree Creek (or Snake Creek) gave way after the pilings were swept away from accumulated tree debris and heavy rains.  Two engines and the first six cars tragically fell into the gully where the bridge had collapsed.  All four crewmen in the engines were killed but the rest of the train was spared thanks to automatic braking.

questions.questionText: What Boston area rail train has been dubbed "A Revolution on Two Wheels"
questions.answerText:
The Minuteman Bikeway was one of the first trails built as a result of "railbanking".  Railbanking is the practice of preserving rail corridors for possible future use, leaving the railroad, railbed, bridges  and other infrastructure intact.

Railbanking the Minuteman Bikeway was proposed in 1974, three years before the railroad closed.  The first section of the bikeway wasn't opened until 1992.    Today the trail stretches 10 miles from Bedford to the Alewife station.

questions.questionText: What West Virginian hall-of-fame rail trail was recognized by Backpacker magazine as "one of the Top 10 hiking trails in the United States.
questions.answerText: This rail trail follows the path of the abandoned Chesapeake and Ohio Railway's former Greenbrier Division for 77.1 miles, through West Virginia, from North Caldwell to Cass.  The trail features 14  trailheads and 16 primitive campsites.  It is considered to be one of the most scenic trails in the eastern US.   This rail trail is appreciated by bikers and hikers alike.
questions.answerImageCaption: Sharps Tunnel on the Greenbriar River Trail

questions.questionText: What New York rail trail can be accessed via elevator
questions.answerText: The High Line is a public park built on a historic freight rail line elevated above the streets on Manhattan’s West Side. Saved from demolition by neighborhood residents and the City of New York, the High Line opened in 2009 as a hybrid public space where visitors experience nature, art, and design.
questions.answerImageCaption: Looking north on the Highline toward the IAC Building, Manhattan, New York

quizzes.title: American Rail Trails
quizzes.subtitle: The USA's best bike routes
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What western rail trail was once named the John Wayne Pioneer Trail?
questions.answerText: The Palouse to Cascades State Park Trail, formerly known as the John Wayne Pioneer Trail and the Iron Horse Trail, is a rail trail that spans most of the U.S. state of Washington. It follows the former railway roadbed of the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul & Pacific Railroad  for 300 miles  across two-thirds of the state, from the western slopes of the Cascade Mountains to the Idaho border.
questions.answerImageCaption: John Wayne Pioneer Trail - east end of Iron Horse park

questions.questionText: At 240 miles, what Missouri rail trail is currently the longest in the USA?
questions.answerText:
The Katy trail follows the right-of-way of the former Missouri–Kansas–Texas Railroad for 240 miles,  across the state of Missouri between the cities of St. Louis and Kansas City.

The nickname "Katy" comes from the phonetic pronunciation of "KT", a short form of the railroad's abbreviated name, MKT. Sections of the Katy are also part of the Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail and the American Discovery Trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: Evening at Katy Trail, Columbia, Missouri

questions.questionText: What project-in-progress will be the longest rail trail in the USA upon completion?
questions.answerText:
Stretching across norther Nebraska, when complete, the Cowboy Trail will run from Chadron to Norfolk, a length of 321 miles, making it the longest rails-to-trails conversion in the United States. It is Nebraska's first state recreational trail.

A short-line railroad (the Nebkota Railway) operated on the westernmost 74 miles of the Cowboy Trail  until 2007.
questions.answerImageCaption: Cowboy Trail Valentine trestle from US 1

questions.questionText: What popular rail trail meets the Appalachian Trail in Damascus, Virginia?
questions.answerText:
This 35 mile scenic trail follows the path of the Abingdon Coal and Iron Railroad.  Trains ran on the tracks until 1977 when flood damage forced the rail company to abandon service.   The US Forest Service removed the tracks and designated it for recreational use.  

Today, shuttles take cyclists to Abington, where cyclists descend to Damascus, passing over several restored trestles on the way.
questions.answerImageCaption: Riders stop at one of the high trestles on the Virginia Creeper Trail.

questions.questionText: What 15 mile Idaho rail trail features 10 tunnels and 7 trestles?
questions.answerText:
The Route of the Hiawatha was one of the first "Hall of Fame" trails designated by the Rail-to-Trail Conservancy.

Originally built as an extension of the Milwaukee Railroad in 1907, The route of the Hiawatha took 9000 men and 234 million to construct due to the challenging terrain.   The railroad was rarely profitable and the last train traversed its tracks in 1961.

quizzes.title: Tall Trail Tales
quizzes.subtitle: Spirits, Critters and Myths from the Trail
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What mythical creature, known for circling mountains in only one direction, has a trail on Mount Mansfield named for it?
questions.answerText:
Part deer, part boar, the wampahoofus (also known as a sidehill gouger)  is famous for circling whatever mountain it lives on, in only one direction.  Consequently, its uphill legs are shorter than its downhill legs.  

Hikers with designs on spotting this elusive creature, might try hiking Vermont's Wampahoofus Trail on Mount Mansfield.
questions.answerImageCaption: Artist rendering of the fabled Wampahoofus, AKA Sidehill Gouger

questions.questionText: What legendary bird nests near the northern end of the Appalachian Trail on Mount Katahdin?
questions.answerText:
Pamola is a legendary bird spirit that appears in Abenaki mythology. This spirit causes cold weather.

Specifically, according to the Penobscot tribal nation, Pamola inhabited Katahdin, the tallest mountain in Maine. Pamola is said to be the thunder god and protector of the mountain. The Penobscot people describe him as having the head of a moose, the body of a man and the wings and feet of an eagle. Pamola was both feared and respected by the Penobscot nation, and his presence was one of the main reasons that climbing the mountain was considered taboo.

The spirit resented mortals intruding from down below. Because of this, the mountain was closed off limits to all below. Henry David Thoreau, of his August, 1846 exploration of the Penobscot River and Katahdin wrote, "Pomola is always angry with those who climb to the summit of Ktaadn."

questions.questionText: The Appalachian Trail passes over what North Carolina mountain, that is also the scene of a Cherokee myth?
questions.answerText: The Cherokee name for Standing Indian Mountain is Yunwitsule-nunyi, which translates to "where the man stood." According to Cherokee mythology, Standing Indian Mountain is home to the remains of a Cherokee warrior. This warrior had been sent to the mountaintop to keep a lookout for a winged monster. The monster, whose lair was located on Standing Indian Mountain, would swoop in from the skies and steal children. Upon discovering the location of the monster's lair, the Cherokee prayed to the Great Spirit for assistance. The prayers were answered when the Great Spirit destroyed the monster and its lair with thunder and lightning. The lightning frightened the warrior and the warrior tried to abandon his post. Because the warrior abandoned his post, he was turned into stone for his cowardice.

questions.questionText: What elusive humanoid are you most likely to encounter while hiking the AT through West Virginia?
questions.answerText:
Mothman, in West Virginian folklore, is a humanoid creature reportedly seen in the Point Pleasant area from November 15, 1966, to December 15, 1967. The first newspaper report was published in the Point Pleasant Register, dated November 16, 1966, titled "Couples See Man-Sized Bird ... Creature ... Something". The national press soon picked up the reports and helped spread the story across the United States. The source of the legend is believed to have originated from sightings of out-of-migration sandhill cranes or herons. 

The creature was introduced to a wider audience by Gray Barker in 1970, and was later popularized by John Keel in his 1975 book The Mothman Prophecies, claiming that there were paranormal events related to the sightings, and a connection to the collapse of the Silver Bridge. The book was later adapted into a 2002 film, starring Richard Gere.

An annual festival in Point Pleasant is devoted to the Mothman legend.
questions.answerImageCaption: Mothman statue located in Point Pleasant, West Virginia

questions.questionText: What dog like beast stalks hikers along the Appalachian Trail on Maryland's South Mountain?
questions.answerText:
The Snarly Yow is a folklore legend that describes a mysterious giant black dog, who has been sighted on South Mountain in Maryland.  Though often associated with local events occurring during the Civil War, the "snarly yow," seems to have originated with 18th century German immigrant folk tales. 

A plaque near South Mountain recounts the story. 

Legend has it that the shadow of a black dog used to prowl the heights of South Mountain.  One night, a huntsman, famous as a sure shot, encountered the beast.  he aimed and fired his rifle.  The shot went right through the animal with no effect.  he fired again and again, each shot passing through the shadowy beast.  Finally, overcome with dread, the huntsman fled.

quizzes.title: International Trails
quizzes.subtitle: Famous Trails Around the World
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What trail starts in Maine, ends at Belle Island  in Nova Scotia and includes stretches in Greenland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Scotland, Northern Ireland, Ireland, Isle of Man, Wales, England, Spain, Portugal, and Morocco.
questions.answerText:
The International Appalachian Trail was proposed in 1994 by Richard Anderson, a Maine fisheries biologist, with plans to traverse the portions of the Appalachian Mountains in Maine, New Brunswick, and Quebec that the Appalachian Trail did not cover. Following route selection, construction of the trail took place through the late 1990s.

In 2009, IAT officials met with the British Geological Survey in Scotland to discuss whether to extend the IAT to the Appalachian terrains of Scotland, Ireland, Northern Ireland, and Wales, setting off a series of expansions through Europe and Northern Africa in which existing trails co-branded with the IAT.

questions.questionText: What famous footpath running through France, Spain, and Portugal was established in the 9th century?
questions.answerText: Created after the discovery of the relics of Saint James the Great at the beginning of the 9th century, the Camino de Santiago, or Way of St. James became a major pilgrimage route of medieval Christianity from the 10th century onwards. But it was only after the end of the Granada War in 1492, under the reign of the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand II of Aragon and Isabella I of Castile, that Pope Alexander VI officially declared the Camino de Santiago to be one of the "three great pilgrimages of Christendom", along with Jerusalem and the Via Francigena to Rome.  Many still follow its routes as a form of spiritual path or retreat for their spiritual growth. It is also popular with hikers, cyclists, and organized tour groups.
questions.answerImageCaption: Sculpture of the pilgrimage route to Santiago de Compostela

questions.questionText: What is the longest trail in the world?
questions.answerText: The Trans Canada Trail, officially named The Great Trail is a cross-Canada system of greenways, waterways, and roadways that stretches from the Atlantic to the Pacific to the Arctic oceans. The trail extends over 15,000 mi (24,000 km).  It is the longest recreational, multi-use trail network in the world. The idea for the trail began in 1992, shortly after the Canada 125 celebrations.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Trans Canada Trail in Onatario

questions.questionText: What British trail was featured in Raynor Winn's prize-winning book "The Salt Path"?
questions.answerText:
The South West Coast Path is England's longest waymarked long-distance footpath and a National Trail. It stretches for 630 miles (1,014 km), running from Minehead in Somerset, along the coasts of Devon and Cornwall, to Poole Harbour in Dorset. 

"The Salt Path" recounts the story of Winn and her husband Moth who was diagnosed with corticobasal degeneration.  The pair became homeless after a business deal with a friend went wrong and decided to walk the 630-mile South West Coast Path.

Described as "An absolutely brilliant story that needs to be told about the human capacity to endure and keep putting one foot in front of another.", the Salt Path won the inaugural RSL Christopher Bland Prizeand was a number one bestselling book in the UK. In 2023, a film adaptation of the same name began filming with Gillian Anderson and Jason Isaacs in the lead roles.
questions.answerImageCaption: Start of the Shouth West Coast Path in Minehead

questions.questionText: What Japanese footpath features 88 Buddhist temples?
questions.answerText: The Shikoku Pilgrimage is a 750-mile, multi-site pilgrimage of 88 temples associated with the Buddhist monk Kūkai on the island of Shikoku, Japan.  The pilgrams, known as henro, are a popular and distinctive feature of the island's cultural landscape. They can be recognized by their white clothing, sedge hats, and wooden walking sticks. Alms or osettai are frequently given to pilgrims by Shikoku's residents.
questions.answerImageCaption: Pilgrims at Zentsū-ji, Temple 75 and the birthplace of Kūkai

quizzes.title: These Trails Weren't Made for Walking
quizzes.subtitle: Trails For Alternative Locamotion
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What popular bike trail runs from Pittsburgh to Cumberland Maryland
questions.answerText:
The Great Alleghany Passage (GAP) runs for 150 miles through Pennsylvannia and Maryland where it meets the terminus of the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal.  Together, these two trails form a 335 mile bike path connecting Pittsburgh and Washington DC.

The GAP offers cyclysts stunning views of the Appalachian Mountains, the wild Youghiogheny River, and idyllic small towns.  Parts of the GAP are included in the  Potomac Heritage National Scenic Trail, one of eight nationally designated scenic trails.  The trail passes over long viaducts and through long railroad tunnels.

The GAP runs though landscapes rich with American industrial history such as steel, coal and railroads. Two Frank Llyod Wright houses are also close to the trail: Falling Water and  Kentuck Knob.
questions.answerImageCaption: Crossing the Keystone Viaduct on the GAP

questions.questionText: What backcountry ski trail runs the entire length of Vermont?
questions.answerText:
At 311 miles, the Catamount Trail is the longest ski trail in the US. Like the Long Trail, it runs from the Massachusetts to the Canadian border.  

The trail got its start in 1982 when three Vermonters, Steve Bushey, Paul Jarris and Ben Rose, woke up one day and said, "Let’s ski from Massachusetts to Quebec!"  After mapping a proposed route, the three founded the Catamount Trail Association, and organization  dedicated to building, administering and maintaining the Catamount Trail.

questions.questionText: What really wet trail runs for 740 miles through four states and two countries?
questions.answerText: The paddler's version of the Appalachian Trail, the Northern Forest Canoe Trail (NFCT) is a 740-mile marked canoeing trail in the northeastern United States and Canada, extending from Old Forge in the Adirondacks of New York to Fort Kent, Maine. Along the way, the trail also passes through the states and provinces of Vermont, Quebec, and New Hampshire. The trail was opened on June 3, 2006.

questions.questionText: What city turns its canal into a 4.8 mile ice skating trail in the winter?
questions.answerText:
Officially recognized by the Guinness Book of World Records as the largest naturally frozen ice rink in the world.  Since 1971, from the Hartwells Lock station at Carleton University to the locks between the Parliament Buildings and the Château Laurier, Ottawa's Rideau Canal is converted to an ice-skating destination every winter.   

The Rideau Canal Skateway is maintained by the  National Capital Commission. The ice is maintained by crews 24 hours a day, seven days a week.  The snow and ice shavings are cleared off the surface every day and the ice surface is flooded each night with a "water dispersion machine"  to fill in any cracks caused by the contracting and expanding ice.

questions.questionText: What's the longest horse trail in North America?

choices.text: Bright Angel Trail in the Grand Canyon

questions.answerText: The Continental Divide trail was conceived as a trail for both people and horses.  Nearly all the CDT's 3,150 miles are open to horses. However, there are sections that are not passable on horseback due to fences, cattle guards, or difficult terrain.
questions.answerImageCaption: Continental Divide Trail in Weminuche Wilderness

quizzes.title: National Historic Trails
quizzes.subtitle: More Nationally Recognized Trails
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What National Historic Trail has been dubbed "the trail by the sea"?
questions.answerText: The Ala Kahakai National Historic Trail is a 175 mile corridor along the coast of the Island of Hawaii.  It encompasses a network of culturally and historically significant trails, intended to  preserve, protect and interpret traditional Native Hawaiian culture and natural resources.   The longest trail within this corridor is the Ala Loa (lit. The long trail).
questions.answerImageCaption: A section of the Ala Kahakai National Historic Trail

questions.questionText: What dog's epic journey saved a town and forged a path that would become one of the first National Historic Parks?
questions.answerText:
Balto was an Alaskan husky and sled dog belonging to musher and breeder Leonhard Seppala. He achieved fame when he led a team of sled dogs driven by Gunnar Kaasen on the final leg of the 1925 serum run to Nome, in which diphtheria antitoxin was transported from Anchorage, Alaska, to Nenana, Alaska, by train and then to Nome by dog sled to combat an outbreak of the disease.

The Iditarod Trail is the only winter trail in the National Trails System . This National Historic Trail system is comprised of a 1,000-mile main trail between Seward and Nome, and an additional 1,400 miles of side/connecting trails that link communities and historic sites, or provide parallel route.

questions.questionText: What National Historic Trail follows the path of Utah bound American pioneers?
questions.answerText:
The story of the Mormon Trail is rooted in the beginnings of a unique American religion. Important differences between mainstream Christianity and Mormon doctrine quickly emerged, but it was primarily hostilities over land, business, and politics that caused Smith repeatedly to move church headquarters. Driven out of Missouri in 1838, the Mormons finally settled along a bend of the Mississippi River in Illinois. There they established a community they called Nauvoo.

Thousands of converts flocked to Nauvoo, soon making it the largest town in Illinois. Neighbors initially welcomed the orderly, industrious settlers despite their religious differences. But relations gradually soured.

Brigham Young stepped up as Smith’s successor and began planning an orderly, spring 1846 evacuation of some 15,000 faithful to the Great Basin, Mexican-held territory beyond the Rocky Mountains. On March 1, 1846, some 500 Mormon wagons lurched northwesterly across the winter-bare Iowa prairie toward the Missouri River. Their route is the Mormon Trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: Mormon Pioneer Trail Crossing marker somewhere of U.S. 218 in Lee County, Iowa.

questions.questionText: What 18th century westward migration route is both a National Historic Trail and the subject of a video game released in 1971?
questions.answerText:
The Oregon Trail follows the wagon train route that a thousand migrants took from Independence, Missouri to Oregon Country. Amidst an overwhelming chorus of naysayers who doubted their success, the so-called "Great Migration" made it safely to Oregon. 

This uniquely American experience became the subject of a video game release in 1971.

questions.questionText: What National Historic Trail follows the path of the 1965 Voting Rights March?
questions.answerText: Established by Congress in 1996, the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail commemorates the people, events, and route of the 1965 Voting Rights March in Alabama. Led by Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., Black and White non-violent supporters fought for the right to vote in Central Alabama. Today, you can connect with this history and trace the events of these marches along the 54-mile trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Edmund Pettus Bridge on the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail

quizzes.title: National Scenic Trails
quizzes.subtitle: 5 Questions for 11 Trails
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What National Scenic Trail was touted as a "Footpath for the People"?
questions.answerText:
Benton MacKaye was a superb salesman.  To promote his idea of "an Appalachian Trail" it was dubbed "a footpath for the people," a title that still resonates today.  

The AT along with the Pacific Crest Trail were the first trails to be designated "National Scenic Trails."  The National Scenic Trail was created by Congress in 1968 to "promote the preservation of, public access to, travel within, and enjoyment and appreciation of the open-air, outdoor areas and historic resources of the Nation". Today, 11 trails share that honor.
questions.answerImageCaption: Franconia Ridge on the Appalachian Trail

questions.questionText: What National Scenic Trail's high point is only 300 feet above sea level?
questions.answerText:
The Florida Trail begins in Big Cypress National Preserve near Miami and runs for 1500 miles to Fort Pickens at Gulf Islands National Seashore, Pensacola Beach.  While the flattest of all the National Scenic Trails, it is far from easy, as large portions of the trail are flooded during most of the year.

The Florida Trail was designated a National Scenic Trail in 1983.

questions.questionText: Which National Scenic Trail travels for 184.5 miles along a canal towpath?
questions.answerText:
The Potomac Heritage Trail officially became a National Scenic Trail in 1983.  The trail consists of several disconnected stretches that  run through Virginia, Maryland and Pennsylvania.  

The three largest sections are:

1) The Mount Vernon Trail in Virginia, which runs 18 miles  between Rosslyn and Mount Vernon, parallel to the Potomac River.  

2) The Chesapeake and Ohio Canal Towpath, which runs 184.5 miles along the Maryland side of the Potomac River between Georgetown in Washington, D.C., and Cumberland, Maryland.

3) The Laurel Highlands Hiking Trail, running for 70 miles in Pennsylvania between Ohiopyle State Park and the Conemaugh Gorge near Johnstown.

questions.questionText: What National Scenic Trail begins and ends in Wisconsin?
questions.answerText:
The Ice Age Trail is a National Scenic Trail stretching 1,200 miles  through Wisconsin, following  a terminal moraine from the last Ice Age.  A terminal moraine is a debris field that accumulates at the edge of a glacier. 

The Ice Age was added the list of National Scenic Trails in 1980.
questions.answerImageCaption: Western Terminus of the Ice Age Trail

questions.questionText: What is the longest National Scenic Trail?
questions.answerText: At 4,800 miles, the North Country National is the longest of the 11 National Scenic Trails.  It runs from Lake Sakakawea State Park in North Dakota to Maine Junction in Vermont, where it meets both the Appalachian and the Long Trail.  It also runs through :  Minnesota, Wisconsin, Michigan, Ohio, Pennsylvania, and New York.
questions.answerImageCaption: North Country National Scenic Trail Marker

quizzes.title: The AT in ME
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Pine Tree State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: Where can you find the only section of trail where the officially recommended mode of transport is not on foot?
questions.answerText:
The Kennebec River Ferry is the recommended way to cross the longest unbridged river crossing on the AT.  Swift currents and n upstream dam that releases water periodically makes fording the river particularly dangerous. 

As an aid to hikers, the Maine Appalachian Trail Club operates a river ferry from May to September.  The canoe can only carry two hikers, so bottlenecks are common on busy days.

questions.questionText: The AT encounters what watery wonder that lends its name to Maine's official drink?
questions.answerText:
The AT bumps the shoreline of Moxie Pond near Moxie Mountain, just south of the Bald Mountain Brook Shelter.

A Native American word for "Dark Water," the pond lent its name to the soda after Moxie's invention in 1876.  Hated by many, but loved by Mainers, this bitter soda-pop was proclaimed Maine's official drink on May 10, 2005.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Moxie Horsemobile, an advertising gimmick to promote the soda

questions.questionText: Where can hikers challenge themselves on the AT's toughest mile?
questions.answerText: Dubbed "The Killer Mile", Mahoosuc Notch is a mile-long, boulder-strewn  scramble that slows down the fastest hikers.  With 10 foot drops, and pack-removing squeezes, this is one of the most challenging sections of the AT.

questions.questionText: What orange-blazed trail, that shares a few miles with AT, follows the route Benedict Arnold took on his failed invasion of Quebec?
questions.answerText:
In 1775 a small American army of about 1000 soldiers under the command of Col. Benedict Arnold passed over the 13 mile Great Carrying Place between the Kennebec and Dead Rivers.  Col. Arnold and Several Companies crossed West Carry Pond in their bateaux and continued over the steep ridge to the West and on to the Dead River.  They then proceeded through the wilderness to attack Quebec.  

The Appalachian Trail, between Middle and West Carry Ponds, precisely follows the old portage trail.

questions.questionText: What alternate trail, can sure-footed hikers return on after reaching the end of the AT on Mount Katahdin?
questions.answerText:
After reaching the summit of Mount Katahdin, most hikers turn around and hike back via the AT.  However, those who like a challenge can return to the parking lot via the Knife Edge Trail.  

Travelling along a narrow ridgeline, Knife Edge Trail is aptly named.  The views along this treeless trail are 360.

quizzes.title: The AT in NH
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Granite State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What school is home to the oldest collegiate outing club in the US?
questions.answerText:
Hanover NH is home to Dartmouth College, and the Dartmouth Outing Club.   The college was founded 1769,  but it wasn't until December 14, 1909 that the club's first formal meeting took place. The club grew quickly and now has about 1500 members.  

Hanover is the last "walk through" town for north bound thru hikers, where the DOC maintains about 50 miles of the AT.  In 1939, the club built the Moosilauke Ravine Lodge, one of the largest log buildings in New Hampshire.
questions.answerImageCaption: Moosilauke Ravine Lodge, maintained by the DOC

questions.questionText: The longest section of the AT that runs entirely above treeline goes through what mountain range?
questions.answerText: For twelve miles, the AT runs through the Presidential range in New Hampshire's White Mountains.  This section crosses several mountains including: Jackson, Pierce, Eisenhower, Franklin, Monroe, Washington, Clay, Jefferson, and Madison.
questions.answerImageCaption: The Appalachian Trail in the Presidential Range

questions.questionText: What is the oldest continuously maintained, hiking path in America?
questions.answerText: The Crawford Path was originally built during the 1800’s and used as a horse trail over the southern Presidential Range to the summit of Mount Washington. In commemoration of its 175th anniversary, the Crawford Path was designated a National Recreation Trail In 1994. This 8.5 mile spectacularly scenic trail is the oldest, continuously maintained, hiking path in America. The section just north of Mt. Pierce to the summit of Mt. Washington is a segment of the Appalachian Trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: Crawford Path on the Summit of Mount Washington

questions.questionText: What is the highest hut operated by the AMC?
questions.answerText:
Lake of the Clouds is one of 8 Huts managed by the Appalachian Mountain Club in New Hampshire.  Sitting at 5000 feet, just below the summit of Mountain Washington (NH's highest peak), the hut offers stunning views of the Presidentials and the Lake of the Clouds.

The hut started as an emergency shelter, built in 1900 after 2 AMC leaders died in a mid-summer snowstorm.  Fifteen years later, the shelter was rebuilt as a 36 bed hut.

Reservations are required, but thru-hikers are allowed to sleep on the floor, when space permits.

questions.questionText: What's the steepest climb on the Appalachian Trail?

choices.text: Beaver Brook Shelter to Kinsman Notch

questions.answerText:
According to the folks at Far Out, the steepest section of the AT is from Pinkham Notch to the summit of Wildcat E.  This section of trail rises 2000 feet in 1.5 miles, including a 1000-foot foot climb in the first  half mile.

New Hampshire is home to the top four steepest climbs on the AT, which explains why many thru-hikers consider the Whites to be the hardest section of the trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: Pinkham Notch by Jasper Francis Cropsey, 1857.

quizzes.title: The AT in MA
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Bay State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: Where on the AT can hikers visit a "lighthouse"?
questions.answerText:
Mount Greylock is not only  the highest point in Massachusetts, but also home to three historic structures:  Bascom Lodge, Thunderbolt Ski Shelter, and the Veterans War Memorial Tower.

The War Memorial is topped by a translucent globe,  illuminated by 1,500 watt lights.  The light can be seen 70 miles away.

questions.questionText: What other iconic long-distance hiking trail does the AT join at the Massachusetts border?
questions.answerText: The AT and its older cousin, the Long Trail, meet at the Massachusetts border and run together for 105 miles, before parting ways at Maine Junction.

questions.questionText: The AT in MA passes through the last battlefield of what armed uprising?
questions.answerText:
In 1787, tax protestors marched on the federal Springfield Armory in an  attempt to seize weapons and overthrow the government.  The rebellion was put down by the Massachusetts State Militia.  

The federal government was unable to field a force because the Articles of Confederation limited its ability to fund an army.   The rebellion exposed the weakness of the Articles, and precipitated the adoption of the US Constitution.

questions.questionText: What AT shelter, a few miles south of Dalton Mass, is named after a town resident, shelter designer, and long time Massachusetts Appalachian Trail Management Committee Volunteer?
questions.answerText:
Kay Wood began volunteering with the Massachusetts Appalachian Trail Management Committee  in the 1960's.  In the 1980's she began designing the shelters found along the AT in the Massachusetts.   

In 1989, using the trail name "Grandma Kay",  she completed an AT thru-hike at the age of 70.   Kay went on to complete the Long Trail and the highest 100 peaks in New England.

The shelter, near her home in Dalton, was named in her honor.

questions.questionText: In what official AT Trail Community do hikers pass a recreation of the 1235lb cheese wheel given to Thomas Jefferson?
questions.answerText:
The Cheshire Mammoth Cheese was a gift from the town of Cheshire, Massachusetts to President Thomas Jefferson in 1802. The 1,235-pound cheese was created by combining the milk from every cow in the town, and made in a makeshift cheese press to handle the cheese's size. The cheese bore the Jeffersonian motto "Rebellion to tyrants is obedience to God."

The trail-friendly town's homage to fromage is on the AT, in the center of town, directly across the from the Father Tom Campsite, where hikers are welcome to pitch a tent and spend the night.

quizzes.title: The AT in CT
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Constitution State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: Connecticut's highest point lies along the Appalachian Trail on the south slope of what New York mountain?
questions.answerText: The AT  crosses the CT-NY border on its way up  Mount Frissell.  This is the highest spot in Connecticut, although the summit  is in New York.  This high-point is marked with a pile of rocks.
questions.answerImageCaption: The highest point in Connecticut, on Mount Frissell's south slope

questions.questionText: The first wheelchair-accessible section of the AT was built along what Connecticut river?
questions.answerText:
The Appalachian Trail Universal Access Loop is a 1.1 mile section of trail along the Housatonic River in the town of Falls Village. It was the first project of its kind to provide wheelchair access to portions of the AT.  

Since then, four more accessible sections have been built:  Thunder Falls in Vermont, Bear Mountain in New York, Pochuck Creek in New Jersey, and Osborne Farm in Tennessee.

questions.questionText: What enormous meadow offers north-bound hikers a view of the distant Berkshire Mountains?
questions.answerText: The wide open meadows of Rand's View are the first real glimpse north-bound thru-hikers have of the Berkshires in Massachusetts. The lush grasses and expansive views makes this spot a popular destination for day-hikers

questions.questionText: What colossal, stone digit lies along the AT near Racoon Hill?
questions.answerText: The Giant's Thumb is a distinctive stone landmark on the AT in Salisbury CT.

questions.questionText: On what mountain summit sits a monument with the erroneous inscription: "This monument marks the highest ground in Connecticut"
questions.answerText: When the monument was constructed, Bear Mountain was considered the highest point in  Connecticut.  However, in the 1940s, the US Geological Survey determined that the high point, was actually on the nearby Connecticut-Massachusetts border.

quizzes.title: The AT in NY
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Empire State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What horizontally-restricted rock scramble can be avoided by following a sign that says "Easy Way"?
questions.answerText: New York's Lemon Squeezer takes hikers through a narrow opening between large rocks, and then up a challenging rock scramble.  While passable, the 3-foot width requires larger hikers to remove their packs.

questions.questionText: After entering Bear Mountain State Park, what tourist attraction does the AT pass through?

choices.text: The formal gardens of an historic mansion

questions.answerText:
The AT passes directly through the Trailside Museums and Zoo.   The Zoo features native NY wildlife, and several small history and nature museums.

At 125 feet above sea level, the path in front of the bear cage is the lowest point on the AT.

While free, hikers who object to the $1 suggested entrance fee can side-step the zoo via a blue-blazed by-pass trail.
questions.answerImageCaption: Entrance to the Trailside Museums and Zoo

questions.questionText: What town, not far from the AT, features a drive-in movie where thru-hikers can camp and watch a double feature from their tents.
questions.answerText:
The town of Warwick is a favorite resupply stop, in part because the Warwick Drive-In Theater permits thru-hikers to camp on a small hill behind the parking lot.  This grassy perch is the perfect spot to munch popcorn, while watching movies alfresco.

Those who appreciate the hospitatlity can pay it back by helping clean the parking lot in the morning.

questions.questionText: What park features the longest paved section of the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText:
Erosion and overuse were persistent problems on the AT in Bear Mountain State Park, until 2006 when the NY-NJ Trail Conference started one of the most ambitious trail reconstruction programs in AT history.

Over 1000 steps, made from 1000 pound granite blocks were hand-laid by 700 volunteers down the side of mountain.  The project lasted four years and cost over a million dollars.

questions.questionText: What's the name of the only train station on the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText: The Appalachian Trail station is a commuter rail stop on the Metro-North Railroad's Harlem Line, serving campers and hikers destined for the Appalachian Trail, in Pawling, New York. It is the only rail station directly located on the Appalachian Trail. Trains stop on weekends and holidays only.

quizzes.title: The AT in NJ
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Garden State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What New Jersey boardwalk does the AT follow for 1.5 miles?
questions.answerText: The 1.5-mile Pochuck Boardwalk, and its 110-foot long, wooden suspension bridge elevate this section of the Appalachian Trail over what was once a prehistoric lake between the Pochuck and Wawayanda Mountain in northern New Jersey.

questions.questionText: What's the highest point on the AT in New Jersey?
questions.answerText: A mere 0.2 miles from the trail, the High Point monument, on the summit of High Point Mountain, in High Point State Park, is the highest point in New Jersey.  At 1,803 feet it is the third lowest high-point along the AT, just ahead of New York and West Virginia.
questions.answerImageCaption: View of High Point, New Jersey from the shore of Lake Marcia within High Point State Park

questions.questionText: Along what ridgeline does more than half of the AT in New Jersey follow?
questions.answerText: From the Delaware Water Gap, the AT climbs and runs along the top of  Kittatinny Mountain.  Characterized by long ridgelines and wide, fertile valleys, the ridgeline is an extension of the Appalachian Mountains' Valley and Ridge Provence.  Hikers can expect frequent views of the Kittatinny Valley as they trek north-east toward Port Jarvis.

questions.questionText: What glacial lake was the destination of a 1000-person hike in 1967?
questions.answerText:
In 1966, a plan to flood Sunfish Pond and the surrounding forest with a reservoir motivated Casey Kays, a local custodian, to lead 655 protesters on a pilgrimage to the pond.  The hike, and subsequent letter writing campaign, caused Jersey Central Power and Light to donate the land to the state.  These events started the "Save The Sunfish Pond" movement. 

In 1967, Supreme Court Justice, William O. Douglass led a 1000-person hike to Sunfish Pond,  to promote legistlation that would permantly preserve the area.  A plaque near the pond commenorates Douglass and the movement.

Today, the AT runs along the shores of Sunfish Pond, a 44-acre, spring-fed glacial lake near the summit of Kittatinny Mountain.
questions.answerImageCaption: View of Sunfish Pond from the Appalachian Trail

questions.questionText: What trail club runs the Mohican Outdoor Center, a favorite overnight destination for thru-hikers in the Delaware Water Gap?
questions.answerText: The AMC, despite being headquartered in New Hampshire, operates the Mohican Outdoor Center, the southernmost location in its chain of mountain huts.  A 0.4 mile trail leads to the center when hikers can look forward to lakeside cabins, hot meals, canoes, and swimming in Catfish Pond.
questions.answerImageCaption: Mohican Outdoor Center, Delaware Water Gap

quizzes.title: The AT in PA
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Keystone State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What trail challenge awaits thru-hikers in Pine Grove Furnace State Park?
questions.answerText:
After reaching the half-way point on the AT, hikers can have their tummies tested by attempting to eat a half-gallon of Hershey's ice-cream in a single sitting.  Hershey shrunk the size of their tubs years ago, so hikers make up the difference with an extra, hand-scooped pint.

Those who defeat the tub, are honored with a wooden ice-cream spoon, emblazened with the words "Member of Half Gal. Club"

Hikers can pick any flavor, but vanilla is the strategic choice.  All those extra bits found in more exotic flavors will fill you up fast.

questions.questionText: What Superfund site is visible from the AT in Pennsylvania?
questions.answerText:
The trail near Palmerton follows a ridgeline that offers views of the Palmerton Superfund site, an environmental disaster that killed off 2,000 acres of Blue Mountain vegetation. A 33 million ton pile of waste, called the Cinder Bank, was left behind by the New Jersey Zinc Company after it closed its mining operations.

Since 1987, restoration has been slow, but steady.  Acres of once dead land have been turned into parks, ski resorts, and State Game Lands.

questions.questionText: What designated Appalachian Trail Community is home to the Appalachian Trail Conservancy's Visitor Center?
questions.answerText:
The AT runs through Boiling Springs, known for its artesian well springs, that bubble up in the middle of town.  The largest of these springs is known as the "Bubble" and appears to boil.

The town is one of 23 designated Appalachian Trail Communities that lie along the AT.
questions.answerImageCaption: Visitor Center for the Appalachian Trail Conservancy in Boiling Springs, Pennsylvania

questions.questionText: What park is home to the first U.S. museum dedicated to a hiking trail?
questions.answerText:
The Appalachian Trial Museum opened in 2010 in an old grist mill in Pine Grove Furnace State Park.

It features historic signs on loan from the Smithsonian, a recreation of Benton MacKaye's Sky Parlor office, a children's museum, mud from Vermont, and a research library.

The is museum is also home to the AT Hall of Fame

questions.questionText: What's a common gripe about the AT in Pennsylvania?
questions.answerText: The Pennsylvanian section of the AT is said to be "where boots go to die."  Affectionately known as Rocksylvannia, this section, while relatively flat, is considered difficult due to its miles of difficult footing.

quizzes.title: The AT in MD
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Old Line State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What historic canal's towpath serves as the first part of the AT in Maryland for north-bound thru-hikers?
questions.answerText:
After crossing the Potomac River on the Winchester and Potomac Railroad Bridge, hikers enjoy an easy 2-ish mile stroll along the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal towpath.

Construction on the canal began in 1828 with the goal of connecting the Chesapeake Bay with the Ohio River near Pittsburgh.  At its peak, the 184.5 mile canal transported goods between  Washington DC and Cumberland Maryland, until a devastating flood shut it down in 1924.

The canal was purchased by the US Government in 1938 and later became the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park

questions.questionText: What public park along the AT was once one a popular amusement park?
questions.answerText:
In 1877, Western Maryland Railroad president John Mifflin Hood, opened an train station and amusement park on South Mountain along the Maryland border with Pennsylvania.    

Pen Mar Park included a dance pavilion, roller coaster, a 450-person dining hall, photo studio, and a movie theatre.  At its height, it attracted up to 20,000 people each summer.  Later, Hood built an observation tower at nearby High Rock, offering tourists stunning views of the nearby valley.

After the introduction of the automobile, the park went into decline, and closed in 1943.    The observation tower fell into disrepair and was demolished, leaving only the foundation.  

The AT passes through Pen Mar Park, which is now a public park.  High Rock is short side-trail away from the main trail.

questions.questionText: What do thru-hikers attempt when they take on the "Maryland Challenge"?

choices.text: Eat a dozen large, steamed crabs
Hike all 41 Maryland miles in a single day
Take zero days in three Maryland towns

questions.answerText:
the AT follows the South Mountain ridgeline from the C&O Canal to the Mason Dixon Line on the PA/MD border.  These 40-ish miles are some the flattest of the AT.  Those accepting the Maryland challenge attempt to hike the entire state in one day.   

Challenge takers often start well before dawn and finish late at night, leaving little time to do anything but walk fast. 

Those with an appreciation of history and natural beauty may want to forgo the challenge as Maryland offers some the richest history and best views the trail has to offer.   The AT in MD features at least 10 Civil War sites, and 5 class-A, scenic overlooks.  Hikers also pass by an historic canal lock, the first Washington Monument, and the War Correspondents Memorial.  The campgrounds are also top-notch, with some offering hot showers.
questions.answerImageCaption: Finish Line for the Maryland Challenge

questions.questionText: What thru-hiker, and author of "The New Appalachian Trail",  has a shelter named for him near Weverton Cliffs?
questions.answerText:
Ed Garvey's book, "Appalachian Hiker, Adventure of a Lifetime", an account of his 1970 thru-hike, is often credited with popularizing the AT, and kickstarting the rise of thru-hiking.

Garvey was an active proponent of the trail. He helped maintain the trail in the DC area and served as the president of the Potomac Appalachian Trail Club.  As president, he worked to pass the "National Trail Act of 1968", which created the National Scenic Trails System.  The Appalachian and Pacific Crest Trails were the first National Scenic Trails, but the act would later be amended to establish the Continental Divide,  North Country, Ice Age, Florida, and many other trails.

At the a the age of 75, during Garvey's second thru-hike, he couldn't help notice how the trail had changed.   His book, "The New Appalachian Trail", which recounts his journey and his thoughts, was published in 1997.

The Ed Garvey Memorial Shelter on the Appalachian Trail at Weverton Cliffs in Maryland, is named in his honor.

questions.questionText: Which Civil War battlefield does the AT run through?
questions.answerText:
The AT runs through Gathland State Park, the site of the Battle of Crampton's Gap. This was one of three Civil War battles fought during the Battle of South Mountain on September 14, 1862.

Union forces, led by Major General George McClellan, clashed with Confederate troops commanded by General Robert E. Lee. The rugged terrain and defensive positions favored the Confederates, but determined Union assaults ultimately forced them to withdraw. The battle marked a turning point as it delayed Lee's advance into Northern territory and provided the Union with valuable intelligence.
questions.answerImageCaption: Union Corps under General Franklin attacking Confederate troops at Crampton's gap.

quizzes.title: The AT in WV
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Mountain State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: Which historic West Virginia town marks the unofficial half-way point on the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText:
The actual half-way point on the Appalachian Trail is unmarked as it changes each year.  If not the physical midpoint,  Harpers Ferry is the psychological midpoint, because celebrating a 1000+ mile trek is much more fun in a beautiful mountain town than in the middle of the woods.

Located at the confluence of the Potomac and Shendandoah rivers, along the C&O canal and B&O railroad, Harpers Ferry has a long history as a commercial center.  

The town also played an important role in the Civil War.  It was here that John Brown conducted his failed raid.  It was also the northernmost point of control for the Confederate army.   The town changed hands many times during the war.  

Today, most of the town lies within Harpers Ferry National Park and draws many tourists both for its history and natural beauty.

questions.questionText: Which venerable Appalachian Trail organization is headquartered in Harpers Ferry, WV?
questions.answerText:
The Appalachian Trail Conservancy (ATC) is a non-profit organization dedicated to the preservation, management, and promotion of the AT. Established in 1925, the ATC serves as the primary steward of the trail, working tirelessly to protect its natural and cultural resources.  

A stop  at the ATC headquarters is considered a right-of-passage for thru-hikers.  Each year the ATC takes and archives thousands of photos of thru-hikers, in front of the building, giving each official acknowledgement of their accomplishment.

These photos are posted on the Hiker Photo Archive on the ATC website.
questions.answerImageCaption: Taking the traditional picture in front the ATC

questions.questionText: What presidential overlook offers stunning views of three states and two rivers?
questions.answerText:
Jefferson Rock is popular stop on the way to  Harpers Ferry.  It overlooks the confluence of the Potomac and Shendandoah rivers, as well as three states: Maryland, Virginia, and West Virginia. 

It is  named after Thomas Jefferson who stood there  in October 25, 1783, and later declared "the passage of the Patowmac through the Blue Ridge is perhaps one of the most stupendous scenes in Nature."

The upper slab of Jefferson Rock originally rested on a natural stone foundation.  Years of weathering and treasure-seeking tourists chipped away at the foundation and  caused the upper slab to become unstable.  A preservation effort, sometime around 1858, saw the slab hoisted onto four concrete pillars.

questions.questionText: The AT passes by the campus of what former college, founded after the Civil War, to educate former slaves?
questions.answerText:
Following the Civil War, Nathan Brackett and the Freewill Baptists, started a one-room mission school dedicated to providing education to the 30,000 former slaves in the Shendandoah Valley.   Through the hard work of Brackett and his wife, the school  grew to serve 2,500 students along the valley.

The school's success drew the ire of many in the area, but also the attention of John Storer who provided the school with a $10,000 grant.  Storer College, as it was now called, not only provided education, but promoted land ownership, enabling 75% of Harpers Ferry’s black citizens to own real estate.

The school continued to flourish until the Supreme Court's 1945, Brown v. Board decision ended school segregation, and Congress cut funding to the school, diverting funds to public universities without religious affiliations.  Storer College closed its doors in 1955.

Today the campus is part of the Harper's Ferry National Park and an informative stop along the AT.
questions.answerImageCaption: Class at Storer College, 1901 (Nathan Brackett and his wife Louise are the third and second people from the left on the second row)

questions.questionText: What wild fruit, found growing along the AT,  is a West Virginia delicacy?
questions.answerText:
Paw paws are small deciduous trees, native the Eastern United States.  They have broad leaves and produce yellowish to brown fruit.    Paw paw fruits have a sweet, custard-like flavor somewhat similar to bananas or mangoes.  Despite being a native fruit, most Americans have never tasted a paw paw.  While delicious, they are fragile, and don't travel well.

The town of Paw Paw, Paw Paw Bends on the Potomac River,  Paw Paw Creek, and the Paw Paw tunnel on the C&O canal are all named for the fruit.  West Virginia University hosts and annual Paw Paw festival, dedicated to promoting West Virginia's largest native fruit.

Paw Paws ripen in the autumn.  Most northbound hikers (nobos) have passed through before paw paw season,  but  southbound hikers (sobos), might find a tree or two.

quizzes.title: The AT in TN
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Volunteer State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: When thru-hiking the Appalachian Trail,  How many times can you expect to cross the Tennessee border?
questions.answerText:
The AT enters Tennessee at Doe Knob and exits near Damascus, VA.  About 75 miles of trail lie in Tennessee Proper.
 
However, an additional 220 miles zigzag along the Tennessee / North Carolina border.  There are no border markers so an accurate count is impossible.  Let's just say it's a lot.
questions.answerImageCaption: Map of the Appalachian Trail at Clingman's Dome

questions.questionText: Which of these Tennessee Parks does the AT run through?
questions.answerText: From Fontana Dam to Davenport Gap, the Appalachian Trail runs through the Great Smoky Mountains National Park, 72 miles in all.  On average, it takes a week to hike  through the park.

questions.questionText: Which of these Tennessee AT shelters was originally a barn?
questions.answerText:
The Overmountain Shelter, near Roan Mountain, was a popular shelter.  Originally a barn, it was converted into a two-story shelter for hikers.

In 2019 the shelter was closed due to structural issues. Sadly, this iconic shelter is now slated for demolition.

questions.questionText: Which of these country classics is about a Tennessee mountain along the Appalachian Trail?

choices.text: "Fire On The Mountain" by the Marshall Tucker Band
My Tennessee Mountain Home” By Dolly Parton

questions.answerText:
"Rocky Top" was released on Christmas Day in 1967.  This song, about the natural beauty of Tennessee, quickly became popular, reaching No. 33 on the U.S. Country charts.

In the 1970's the University of Tennessee adopted it as their official fight song and is now played at every football game.

In 1982 the general assembly voted to make it the fifth official song of the Tennessee.
questions.answerImageCaption: The AT on Rocky Top in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park

questions.questionText: The Great Smoky Mountains National Park isn't the only federal land in Tennessee the AT crosses.  Which of these national forests can AT hikers wander through?
questions.answerText: The Appalachian Trail runs for about 150 miles through the Cherokee National Forest.  Created in 1920, the forest covers 655,598 acres and is managed by the United States Forest Service.

quizzes.title: The AT in NC
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Tar Heel State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: What grassy summit, offering 360 degree views of Tennessee and North Carolina was closed to camping after a photographer took photos of hundreds of tents and trash piles in 2019?
questions.answerText:
In the 19th century, sheep and cattle ranchers cleared the summit of Max Patch for pasture.  The stunning views they created made the peak a popular destination.  It was the site of an Inn and and hostel in the early 20th century.  To prevent to construction of an airstrip,  the US Forest Service bought the land in 1982. In 1984 the AT was routed over Max Patch making it  a favorite place for thru-hikers to pitch a tent.  

At the start of the Covid pandemic, people flocked to Max Patch.  There are no facilities at Max Patch so sewage and trash quickly accumulated.   Photographer, Mike Wurmann documented the destruction in 2019. Photos of a small tent-city and piles of garbage shocked many in the hiking community.

To protect the native flora and fauna, the USFS banned overnight camping in 2021.
questions.answerImageCaption: Mark Wurmann's 2019 photo of tents on Max Patch

questions.questionText: What North Carolina shelter is favored by thru-hikers for its hot showers and other amenities?
questions.answerText: Affectionately known as the Fontana Hilton, the Fontana Dam Shelter offers electricity, free hot showers, flush toilets, water spigots and a cheap shuttle into Fontana Dam North Carolina.
questions.answerImageCaption: Appalachian Trail Sign, Fontana Dam, NC

questions.questionText: According to Cherokee mythology, what AT peak is home to the remains of a Cherokee warrior?
questions.answerText: The Cherokee name for Standing Indian Mountain is Yunwitsule-nunyi, which translates to "where the man stood." According to Cherokee mythology, Standing Indian Mountain is home to the remains of a Cherokee warrior. This warrior had been sent to the mountaintop to keep a lookout for a winged monster. The monster, whose lair was located on Standing Indian Mountain, would swoop in from the skies and steal children. Upon discovering the location of the monster's lair, the Cherokee prayed to the Great Spirit for assistance. The prayers were answered when the Great Spirit destroyed the monster and its lair with thunder and lightning. The lightning frightened the warrior and the warrior tried to abandon his post. Because the warrior abandoned his post, he was turned into stone for his cowardice.

questions.questionText: What's the first "walk through" town north-bound thru-hikers encounter on the Appalachian Trail?
questions.answerText:
Lying directly on the AT, Hot Springs, NC is an easy re-supply spot for thru-hikers.

The town's natural  thermal springs offer weary hikers a chance to soak tired muscles in 108-degree, mineral waters.
questions.answerImageCaption: AT Sign in Hot Springs, NC

questions.questionText: One of the AT's most recognizable trees grows in North Carolina.  Where can find this tree?
questions.answerText:
Just after crossing from Georgia into North Carolina at Bly Gap, hikers are welcomed by one of the most photographed trees on the AT.  

This gnarled oak tree is instantly recognized by most thru-hikers.

quizzes.title: The AT in VA
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Old Dominion State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: At 550 miles, Virginia has the most Appalachian Trail miles of any state.  Thru-hikers spend about 104 of those miles in which national park?
questions.answerText:
In May 1925, Congress and President Calvin Coolidge authorized the National Park Service to acquire a minimum of 250,000 acres to create Shendandoah National Park.  Congress provided no funds, so  The commonwealth of Virginia slowly acquired the land through eminent domain.   

During the Great Depression, the Civilian Conservation Corps provided much needed jobs and training building Skyline Drive and other park facilities, as well as removing dead chestnut trees.

In 1935, the park was officially established.

questions.questionText: What Virginian road crosses the Appalachian Trail 32 times?
questions.answerText:
The plans for the construction of Skyline Drive drove deep divisions among the founders of the Appalachian Trail.  Benton MacKaye who first proposed the trail saw the road as an intrusion into the wilderness. Chairman of the Appalachian Trail Conference, Myron Avery, supported the project believing that it would make the trail accessible to more people. 

The AT and Skyline Drive run along side each other through Shenandoah National Park, crisscrossing 32 times.

questions.questionText: What Virginia shelter is famous as the only shelter on the AT where hikers can have pizza delivered?
questions.answerText:
The Partnership Shelter lies a short distance from the Pat Jennings Visitor Center.  It's payphone and  location off the Sugar Grove Highway, affords easy access to food delivery.   Hot showers and flush toilets can also be had at the visitor center.

Unfortunately, the shelter's proximity to the road lends the shelter a reputation for being a crowded party spot.

questions.questionText: What Virginian geologic feature has been dubbed the "most photographed spot on the AT"?
questions.answerText:
Located on Catawba Mountain, this geological feature is  named for a Scottish-Irish 18th-century settler.

MacAfee's Knob  offers a panorama view of the Catawba Valley, North Mountain, Tinker Cliffs, and the Roanoke Valley.    

A photograph of yourself, preferably with feet dangling from the precipice, is considered a "right of passage" for thru-hikers.

questions.questionText: What Virginia town hosts the annual "Trail Days" festival?
questions.answerText: Trail Days is an annual festival held in Damascus, Virginia, that celebrates the Appalachian Trail and the hiking community. This multi-day event typically takes place in May and attracts hikers, outdoor enthusiasts, and locals alike. One of the main highlights is the hiker parade, where through-hikers proudly march through the town displaying their trail names, sharing stories, and showcasing the diversity of the hiking community. The festival also features live music, vendors offering outdoor gear, food stalls, and various workshops on hiking-related topics. Trail Days serves as a hub for hikers to come together, share experiences, and build a sense of camaraderie. It has become a beloved tradition that captures the spirit of the Appalachian Trail and the tight-knit community that forms among those who embark on the epic journey.

quizzes.title: The AT in GA
quizzes.subtitle: Trail Facts from the Peach State
quizzes.author: John Need

questions.questionText: The AT isn’t the only famous trail to terminate on Springer Mountain.  What other popular hiking trail meets the AT on Springer Mountain?
questions.answerText: The Benton MacKaye trail is named after the founder of the AT.   It runs for about 300 miles from Big Creek in the Smokey Mountains to Springer Mountain where it shares a terminus.

questions.questionText: Georgia is home to the only part of the AT that is covered.  Where can you stand on the AT during the rain and not get wet?
questions.answerText:
The Walasi-Yi Interpretive Center is a small stone building located at Neels Gap on the eastern side of Blood Mountain. It is notable as the only place where the Appalachian Trail passes through a man-made structure. It is currently the first mail-drop available to northbound thru-hikers that does not require one to leave the trail.
 
Originally a log structure built by a logging company, the building took its present form during the 1930s when it was rebuilt by the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC). It served as a restaurant and inn until 1965, when it was abandoned. Soon after, the building was rented by an artist group who used it until 1969 when it was again left vacant. ￼

By the mid-1970s the building was slated for demolition, but a group of conservation-minded locals lobbied successfully for its inclusion on the National Register of Historic Places in 1977. Spared from destruction, the building served as an irregular store to hikers and tourists.  Today, the building houses a hostel and outdoor outfitter, Mountain Crossings.
questions.answerImageCaption: Walasi-Yi Center, Neels Gap, GA - Appalachian Trail passage

questions.questionText: For most thru-hikers, the Appalachian Trail starts at Springer Mountain in Georgia.  However the AT used to start further south.  What Georgia mountain was the original southern terminus of the AT?
questions.answerText:
Mount Oglethorpe is the southernmost peak in the Blue Ridge Mountains, and was the start of the AT in 1930.

Unfortunately, Mount Oglethorpe is not part of the National Forest.  In the 1950’s much the land was bought by loggers and chicken ranchers.  Over-development and the stench of chicken manure  convinced the Georgia Appalachian Trail Club to move the AT terminus north, to Springer Mountain.

questions.questionText: What this highest point along the AT in Georgia
questions.answerText:
At 1360 meters, Blood Mountain is the highest point of the AT on Georgia. This is considered by many to be would-be thru-hikers first challenge.  

Blood mountain is also home to the Blood Mountain Shelter, an historic, stone hut, built in 1937 by the Civilian Conservation Corps

questions.questionText: What Georgia State park serves as the launching point for most would-be thu-hikers
